sourceText: Gutenberg faced significant financial difficulties in funding his invention and suffered a major setback when he lost a lawsuit to his business partner, Johann Fust, leading to his bankruptcy and the seizure of his printing equipment.

Despite the setbacks of bankruptcy and equipment seizure, Gutenberg persevered, continuing his printing work on a smaller scale and refining his techniques. While his invention's full impact was not realized in his lifetime, it laid the foundation for future advancements in printing technology.
Printing is the light of knowledge.
This means that thanks to printing, knowledge can spread more widely, acting like a lamp illuminating the darkness.
This emphasizes that deep knowledge and truth can be explored through books.
This holds the meaning that through experience and reflection over time, the most valuable lessons are learned.

Despite challenging circumstances, Ibn Sina maintained his passion for scholarship. He produced numerous masterpieces through constant research and writing, and his exceptional medical skills and knowledge earned him respect, becoming a driving force to overcome adversity.

Diogenes turned his exile and social criticism into an opportunity to pursue freedom. He renounced all possessions and pursued inner virtue and self-sufficiency through a simple life, making his very existence a philosophical statement.
From a young age, severe visual impairment and intermittent double vision posed significant challenges to his studies and research, often leading to frustration.

He utilized visual aids, relied on colleagues, and developed unique methods to lecture without directly seeing the blackboard, relentlessly pursuing academic interests despite physical hardships and overcoming adversity.
All existence depends on the way we experience it.
